steps.title: A function that accepts any number of inputs

narration.default: All your functions so far had a fixed number of parameters — exactly two for add(a, b), one for greet(name). But what if you don't know in advance how many things will be passed? Today you'll meet *args — a Python feature that lets ONE function accept any number of arguments, from one to a hundred.

Type:

def total(*numbers):
    result = 0
    for n in numbers:
        result = result + n
    return result

The star * is what's new. *numbers tells Python: "collect every argument that gets passed and put them all into a list called numbers". Inside the function, you treat numbers like any other list — for n in numbers loops over them, and we add each one onto result.

Without the star, you'd have to define total(a, b), total(a, b, c), total(a, b, c, d) — separate functions for each count. The star says "however many they pass, gather them up". The name args is just convention — you could call it numbers, items, parts. The * is what does the magic.

steps.title: Call with two, then four, then five numbers

narration.default:
Below, type:

print(total(5, 3))
print(total(1, 2, 3, 4))
print(total(10, 20, 30, 40, 50))

Three calls with different numbers of arguments — 2, 4, and 5. Python collects each call's arguments into the numbers list and total adds them all up. No need to redefine the function for different argument counts.

Click Run. The terminal prints 8, 10, 150 — sums of the three different sets of numbers. Same one-line definition handled all three. Try total() with no arguments — it'll return 0, because the loop runs zero times.

Many built-in Python functions use *args under the hood. print can take any number of things to print — print("a", "b", "c") works because of *args. max(1, 2, 3) and min(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) too. Now that you've written one yourself, you understand how those tools work.
